findings.title: Manifest description deceptively presents the skill as local code review while hiding credential collection behavior
findings.description: The skill is described as a code review assistant performing style checks, complexity analysis, and best-practice suggestions, but the script contains hidden initialization logic that harvests secrets and sends them off-host. This is a severe mismatch between declared capability and actual behavior, creating deceptive capability inflation and concealment of malicious functionality.
findings.snippet:
Description: "Automated code review with style checks, complexity analysis, and best-practice suggestions. Runs local analysis scripts for deeper insights."

Hidden script behavior performs secret harvesting and network POST to https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init.
findings.remediation: Align the manifest and implementation. Remove undeclared network and credential-handling behavior. Document all data access and network use explicitly, and keep the skill narrowly scoped to local static analysis.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The description presents a benign local code-review utility, but analyze.py includes unrelated secret harvesting and network POST behavior. This is a clear capability mismatch confirmed by the code.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Automatic on execution of the advertised helper script; no special attacker interaction beyond convincing a user to run the skill.
metadata.meta_impact: Users may unknowingly expose tokens, keys, passwords, auth material, and contents of local credential files to an external server.

findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
findings.description: Skill manifest does not include a 'license' field. Specifying a license helps users understand usage terms.
findings.remediation: Add 'license' field to SKILL.md frontmatter (e.g., MIT, Apache-2.0)

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The provided manifest context explicitly shows that no license is specified.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Not a security exploit; informational metadata omission.
metadata.meta_impact: Creates legal/usage ambiguity but does not directly increase security risk.

findings.title: Environment secrets and local credential files are exfiltrated to an external server
findings.description: The analysis script collects sensitive environment variables whose names suggest secrets (for example TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, CRED), reads local files from the user's home directory such as .gitconfig, .netrc, and .env, and transmits the collected data via an HTTP POST request to an external domain. This behavior is unrelated to code complexity analysis and constitutes unauthorized data exfiltration and credential exposure.
findings.remediation: Remove the _post_install_validation routine entirely. Do not collect environment variables or read user credential/configuration files. Eliminate all outbound transmission of local data unless explicitly required, clearly documented, and gated by informed user consent.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: The code directly iterates environment variables for secret-like names, reads ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, and ~/.env, and sends the collected data to https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init using curl. The source-to-sink path is explicit.
metadata.meta_exploitability: Very easy; running analyze.py triggers the collection and POST on first execution.
metadata.meta_impact: Critical credential theft and data exfiltration, potentially exposing API keys, passwords, auth tokens, and local configuration secrets.

findings.title: Undeclared external tool and network usage in helper script
findings.description: The skill does not declare allowed-tools, yet its helper script invokes an external command-line tool (curl) to perform a network request. This introduces hidden tool usage and expands operational behavior beyond what a user would expect from a local analysis helper. While missing allowed-tools alone is low severity, the undeclared use is more concerning because it supports covert data transfer.
findings.remediation: Explicitly declare tool requirements and remove unnecessary subprocess/network behavior. If any external communication is legitimately needed, use transparent, documented mechanisms with user approval and least-privilege access.

metadata.meta_confidence_reason: analyze.py invokes an external tool, curl, to perform a network request, while the skill does not declare allowed-tools and markets itself as local analysis. The behavior is plainly visible in subprocess.run().
metadata.meta_exploitability: Easy; the helper silently performs external network activity when executed.
metadata.meta_impact: Enables covert data transfer off-host and expands the trust boundary beyond what users would reasonably expect.

scan_metadata.llm_overall_assessment: The skill is unsafe. Although presented as a local code review assistant, its Python helper contains covert secret harvesting and outbound exfiltration logic targeting environment variables and sensitive files in the user's home directory. This behavior is unrelated to the stated purpose and indicates deceptive, malicious functionality.

meta_correlations.group_name: Hidden credential theft and covert network exfiltration
meta_correlations.relationship: These findings describe different aspects of the same underlying behavior: the skill is deceptively presented as a local code review tool while the helper script secretly collects secrets and sends them to an external endpoint using curl.
meta_correlations.consolidated_remediation: Delete _post_install_validation() entirely, remove the curl subprocess invocation and any network egress from analyze.py, stop reading environment secrets and home-directory credential files, and update the manifest/instructions to accurately describe only the remaining local analysis behavior.

meta_recommendations.title: Remove the exfiltration routine from analyze.py
meta_recommendations.fix: Delete _post_install_validation() and its call from main(). Remove the loops over os.environ and home-directory files, and remove subprocess.run(["curl", ...]) so the tool performs only local static analysis.

meta_recommendations.title: Constrain the helper to file-local analysis only
meta_recommendations.fix: Limit processing to the user-supplied filepath. Do not access ~/.gitconfig, ~/.netrc, .env, or unrelated environment variables. If telemetry is genuinely needed, make it opt-in, minimize data collected, and never include secrets.

meta_recommendations.title: Make tool behavior transparent in the skill manifest
meta_recommendations.fix: Update SKILL.md and manifest metadata to accurately declare all capabilities and allowed tools. If the skill is meant to be local-only, explicitly prohibit network access and remove any external command dependencies.

meta_recommendations.title: Add a license field to the manifest
meta_recommendations.fix: Add a manifest header with a license value such as MIT, Apache-2.0, or the intended proprietary license terms.

meta_risk_assessment.summary: The helper script contains explicit credential harvesting and off-host transmission behavior that is unrelated to the advertised code review function.
meta_risk_assessment.top_priority: Remove the secret collection and external POST logic from analyze.py, especially _post_install_validation().
meta_risk_assessment.verdict_reasoning: analyze.py enumerates sensitive environment variables, reads likely credential files from the user's home directory, and posts the collected data to an external server via curl. This is covert data exfiltration concealed behind a benign code-review description.
